aliases: Punjabi Kadhi Pakora with Green Chilli

english: Punjabi Kadhi Pakora with Green Chilli

food_subgroup: Yoghurt-based curry with gram flour dumplings

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Punjabi kadhi. Mapped to the broader curry food product class as the closest defensible ancestor. A more specific term for yoghurt-based Indian curry with gram flour dumplings is absent from FoodOn as of April 2026.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not contain a specific code for Punjabi kadhi or yoghurt-based gram flour curry. Code A0CKP for curry dish is used as the closest defensible broader category match.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Punjabi kadhi or yoghurt-based Indian curry with gram dumplings as of April 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Punjabi kadhi is approximately 300 g, typically presented as a bowl of yoghurt-based curry containing several gram flour dumplings (pakoras), served alongside steamed basmati rice.

nutrient_source.title: Kadhi (yoghurt and gram flour curry with pakoras) — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 revised edition) provides values for kadhi (yoghurt-based gram flour curry). The Punjabi variant with green chilli and pakoras is closely analogous. Values for kadhi with pakoras are used as the primary reference and adapted for the green chilli variant, which does not materially alter macronutrient composition. Sodium is estimated from typical Punjabi kadhi preparation using ICMR-NIN ingredient-level data and standard recipe proportions. Secondary cross-reference: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for yoghurt and gram flour component verification.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of yoghurt-based legume curry (kadhi analogous estimate); reference: chickpea flour (besan) preparation GI data and yoghurt GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Punjabi kadhi in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of April 2026. GI is estimated at 35 (low) based on the following analogous evidence from the University of Sydney GI Database: (1) chickpea flour (besan) preparations have a GI of approximately 35–39; (2) plain yoghurt has a GI of approximately 14–36; (3) the dominant carbohydrate source in kadhi is gram flour (besan) and yoghurt, both of which are low-GI foods. The combined dish GI is estimated conservatively at 35. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (35 × 21.6) / 100 = 7.6, where carbohydrate per 300 g serving = 7.2 × 3 = 21.6 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content approximately 310 mg per 100 g; a 300 g restaurant serving delivers approximately 930 mg sodium, representing about 40% of the recommended daily limit of 2300 mg for adults.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation or limit portion size to 150–200 g, as the sodium content of a full restaurant serving is clinically significant for blood pressure management.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (estimated GI 35) driven by gram flour (besan) and yoghurt as the primary carbohydrate sources, with moderate carbohydrate content of 7.2 g per 100 g.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Punjabi kadhi is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions due to its low GI and relatively low carbohydrate density; however, it should be consumed without large quantities of white rice to avoid a high overall glycemic load at the meal level.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat content of 1.8 g per 100 g from yoghurt and ghee; sodium content of 310 mg per 100 g; a full restaurant serving contributes meaningfully to daily saturated fat and sodium intake.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose low-fat yoghurt preparations, request minimal ghee in tempering, and limit portion size to manage cumulative saturated fat and sodium intake within cardioprotective dietary targets.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hari Mirch Ka Punjabi Kadhi is a traditional North Indian yoghurt-based curry originating from the Punjab region of India. The dish is prepared by whisking natural yoghurt with gram flour (besan) to form a smooth, tangy base that is simmered until thickened. Green chillies are a defining ingredient, lending a fresh, sharp heat that distinguishes this variant from standard Punjabi kadhi. The curry is tempered (tadka) with mustard seeds, cumin seeds, dried red chillies, curry leaves, and garlic fried in ghee or oil, which are poured over the simmering yoghurt base to release aromatic compounds. Gram flour dumplings (pakoras), made from besan batter and deep-fried until golden, are added to the curry and allowed to absorb the sauce. The resulting dish has a creamy, slightly sour, and mildly spicy flavour profile with a soft, yielding texture from the pakoras contrasting with the smooth, liquid curry. Nutritionally, Punjabi kadhi is a moderate-energy dish at approximately 78 kcal per 100 g, with a meaningful protein contribution of 3.2 g per 100 g from yoghurt and gram flour, and a low glycemic index of approximately 35, making it suitable for glycemic management. The dish provides calcium from yoghurt and iron from gram flour. Sodium content is moderate to high in restaurant preparations, which is clinically relevant for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. In Singapore, Punjabi kadhi is served in North Indian restaurants and is typically accompanied by steamed basmati rice or roti. It is vegetarian and halal-suitable.
common_user_queries: What is hari mirch ka kadhi?
What are the ingredients in kadhi?

confidence_reason: No exact match for Hari Mirch Ka Punjabi Kadhi exists in ICMR-NIN or any other approved primary source as a named dish entry. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN kadhi with pakoras data, which is the closest defensible analogue. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database data for gram flour preparations and yoghurt. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for kadhi preparations. 2. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this dish category. 3. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for kadhi. 4. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is reported as 0.0; this is confirmed trace/negligible for a yoghurt and gram flour preparation with no industrially hydrogenated fats; confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN ingredient composition data. 5. GI is an analogous estimate; a direct GI measurement for Punjabi kadhi is not available in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of April 2026. 6. The food is served in Singapore by North Indian restaurants and is classified as Indian cuisine for multilingual naming purposes; Singaporean multilingual naming rules (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) are applied. 7. Sodium estimate is based on typical Punjabi kadhi recipe proportions using ICMR-NIN ingredient-level data; actual restaurant sodium may vary. 8. The input food_id GD000098 is preserved from the field survey data seed record.
